EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused blizzard conditions over southwest Minnesota, mainly away from the southern border of the state. The strong winds combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall. The new snowfall varied mostly from 2 to 4 inches. Zero visibilities and drifting snow made travel virtually impossible. Wind chill readings were driver to as low as 40 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds sustained around 30 mph with gusts over 40 mph combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of 2 inches or less to produce blowing snow, with brief visibilities around a quarter mile in places. Temperatures falling to near or a little below zero combined with the strong winds to produce wind chill readings of 20 to 35 below zero.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down approximately 2.5 miles Southeast of downtown Becker, near the intersection of Highway 10 and 137th Street. It tracked East-Northeast for four miles. Damage included: broken trees, destroying a few sheds and causing minor house damage. The tornado dissipated just West of Eagle Lake, or about 3.5 miles North of Big Lake. In addition, just to the South of the tornado track, there was some straight line wind damage as well. This occurred most likely from a rear flank downdraft that accompanied the tornado.

Low pressure moved from the central Plains toward the Ohio Valley and produced widespread snow across the Upper Midwest on January 21. In southeast Minnesota, highest snowfall totals reached around 8 inches. Specific reports from weather observers included 8.2 inches near La Crescent (Houston County) and 8.0 inches near Houston (Houston County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall caused difficult traveling conditions, which contributed to a fatal accident on Interstate 90 involving a semi-trailer and a van.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through during the afternoon and early evening hours of December 14th. With the frontal passage, temperatures dropped as much as 20 degrees in 2 to 3 hours at many locations. In fact, a temperature change of around 40 degrees was common from the morning to the evening hours of December 14th.||The cold was made more significant as northwest winds increased into the 20 to 30 mph range, with gusts of 35 to 45 mph. These strong winds, coupled with plummeting temperatures, created dangerous wind chills of 20 below to 40 below. Bitter cold conditions persisted through the day and into the evening on December 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lowest unofficial wind chill readings included -34F at Canton and Preston with peak wind gusts of 38 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced three tornadoes and a report of damaging winds in southwest Minnesota at the end of the afternoon on June 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado, after crossing into Murray County from Nobles County, destroyed a garage and a barn, blew down a grain bin, damaged several sheds, and caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced three tornadoes and a report of damaging winds in southwest Minnesota at the end of the afternoon on June 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado blew the roof off a house, collapsed a wall in the house, and shifted the house off its foundation. A woman taking shelter in a closet was injured. On the same farmstead, the tornado also destroyed two sheds and caused minor damage to another house and two outbuildings. The tornado carried some debris from the farm over a mile. The tornado also caused extensive tree damage.

A weak area of surface low pressure tracked from near Cooperstown (ND) to near Thief River Falls (MN) by the early evening of the 13th. A cold front moved through Mahnomen County as the surface low passed to the north. Once again, cold temperatures aloft helped contribute to the formation of a few thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief cold core funnel was viewed south of Mahnomen. No touchdown was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front ushered in very strong and damaging northwest winds across west central Minnesota. Northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph began in the morning and continued into the early evening. The high winds downed some trees and branches, damaged some roofs, fences, and signs, along with bending over some unharvested corn.

A strong cold front whipped through Central and Southern Minnesota on this date. Strong Northwest winds behind the front were most prevalent across a portion of West Central and much of South Central Minnesota. Some of the highest gusts that occurred were: 58 knots five miles South of Marietta; 54 knots at Blue Earth, 53 knots in Hanley Falls; 52 knots in Fairmont; 50 knots at Redwood Falls; 49 knots in Alexandria; 48 knots at Morris, 48 knots in Glenwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifty knot gust occurred in Redwood Falls at 0710.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After being hit by heavy snow December 18 and 19, southeast Minnesota was impacted by another winter storm December 20 through 21. Highest snowfall amounts were generally 4 to 7 inches, with specific reports of 7.6 inches at Preston (Fillmore County), 7.0 inches near Rochester International Airport (Olmsted County) and 6.3 inches at Grand Meadow (Mower County).||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph in the wake of an arctic cold front produced near white-out conditions, along with 2 to 4 foot drifts. In addition to the wind and snow, sharply falling temperatures resulted in bitterly cold wind chills of 20 below to 40 below, which lingered into December 22.||Due to several in a series of snow-producing systems, December 2008 would turn out to be the third snowiest December on record in Rochester, with a total of 28.6 inches. The all-time December snowfall record in Rochester remained at 35.3 inches, which was established in 2000.

Strong Northwest winds behind the front were most prevalent across a portion of West Central and much of South Central Minnesota. Some of the highest gusts that occurred were: 58 knots five miles South of Marietta; 54 knots at Blue Earth, 53 knots in Hanley Falls; 52 knots in Fairmont; 50 knots at Redwood Falls; 49 knots in Alexandria; 48 knots at Morris, 48 knots in Glenwood.

Some of the highest totals included 9.0 inches at Rushford (Fillmore County), 8.5 inches at Dodge Center (Dodge County) and 8.0 inches near La Crescent (Houston County).

A surface boundary moved into the central Dakotas, with a very moist air mass in place ahead of it. Precipitable water values were about 200 percent of normal. As the low level jet increased in the evening, thunderstorms fired along the boundary. The most rain fell in a thin ribbon from just east of Devils Lake up toward Pembina (ND).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 6 to 8 inch diameter tree limbs were blown down in a shelterbelt.

An arctic cold front moved through during the afternoon and early evening hours of December 14th. With the frontal passage, temperatures dropped as much as 20 degrees in 2 to 3 hours at many locations. In fact, a temperature change of around 40 degrees was common from the morning to the evening hours of December 14th.||The cold was made more significant as northwest winds increased into the 20 to 30 mph range, with gusts of 35 to 45 mph. These strong winds, coupled with plummeting temperatures, created dangerous wind chills of 20 below to 40 below. Bitter cold conditions persisted through the day and into the evening on December 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On December 14th, the temperature at the Austin Municipal Airport dropped from 39F at noon to -2F at 9 p.m. In addition, a peak wind gust of 45 mph was recorded. The lowest wind chill readings were -36F during the early morning hours of December 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of surface low pressure deepened as it moved from Eastern South Dakota to West Central Minnesota during the morning and afternoon hours. This system was responsible for strong winds across portions of East Central Minnesota during the late morning and or the early afternoon. Some wind gusts of note include: 55 knots at Stanton Airport; 52 knots at St. Paul Airport; 46 knots at South St. Paul; 45 knots recorded at Humboldt Senior High School 2 miles Southeast of St. Paul; 43 knots in Eden Prairie. Trees down and townhomes damaged near Baker Road and Highway 62 in Eden Prairie. Roof collapse reported 8555 Edenburgh Center Drive in Brooklyn Park. The roof collapse led to a gas leak in the home. Scattered trees and power lines toppled in Faribault.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent surface low pressure system moved out of Colorado late Saturday (13th) and tracked northeast to the Minneapolis area by noon on Sunday (14th). This created a strong temperature gradient across the northern plains, with Devils Lake (ND) at 15 below zero and the Minneapolis (MN) area around 30 above by noon Sunday. As the system intensified over eastern Minnesota, northwest winds began to gust to around 50 mph with wind chills colder than 40 below zero. Quite a bit of snow also accompanied the wind, which created whiteout conditions for an extended period of time. A blizzard this bad had not been seen since the winter of 1996/97, so the impact on the area was tremendous. Stores closed for portions of the weekend during the busy holiday shopping season. Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown (ND) to Alexandria (MN). U.S. Highway 10 was closed from Moorhead to Detroit Lakes and U.S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston. No travel was advised across the area. Church services, schools, and many other activities were cancelled or delayed. There were other minor power outages across the area as well.

An arctic cold front and a strong area of low pressure that developed across the northern Rockies and moved eastward across the upper Midwest and into the northern Great Lakes, brought heavy snow, very strong winds and blizzard conditions across a large portion of western and central Minnesota. A broad area of six inches (Locally (9+ inches occurred near the communities of Little Falls and Hewitt in central Minnesota) or more fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota on Sunday December 15th. The heaviest snowfall occurred Sunday morning where periods of very heavy snowfall fell across a large area of west central and central Minnesota. In addition to the snowfall, severe wind chills of 30 to 50 below zero occurred across the region as wind speeds increased and temperatures dropped well below zero. The hardest hit areas were across west central Minnesota where significant snowfall and wind gusts in excess of 40 mph produced blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the Alexandria area between 6 and 8 pm Saturday (December 13th )evening as low pressure started to intensify across the northern Plains. Light snow continued through the night and became heavy between 8 am and 10 am on Sunday. In addition, northwest winds began to increase with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 35 to 39 mph between 10 am and lasted through Sunday evening. A peak wind gust of 39 mph occurred at the Alexandria airport at 1020 pm Sunday evening.|During the overnight hours of Sunday/Monday December 14/15, between midnight and 9 am, wind chill values averaged between 35 to 40 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm frontal boundary over Southwest MN. was the mechanism for the development of isolated severe weather on this date.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the Glenwood area between 6 and 8 pm Saturday (December 13th )evening as low pressure started to intensify across the northern Plains. Light snow continued through the night and became heavy between 8 am and 10 am on Sunday. In addition, northwest winds began to increase with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 35 to 40 mph between 830 am and lasted through Sunday evening. The worst conditions occurred after 6 pm Sunday where surface visibilities were less then 200 feet. Snowfall amounts across Pope County were between four and six inches. During the overnight hours of Sunday/Monday December 14/15, between midnight and 9 am, wind chill values averaged between 35 to 45 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent surface low pressure system moved out of Colorado late Saturday (13th) and tracked northeast to the Minneapolis area by noon on Sunday (14th). This created a strong temperature gradient across the northern plains, with Devils Lake (ND) at 15 below zero and the Minneapolis (MN) area around 30 above by noon Sunday. As the system intensified over eastern Minnesota, north to northwest winds increased and caused some blowing and drifting snow along with very cold wind chills. Four to 10 inches of snow fell from Roseau to Baudette to Bemidji to Wadena.

A strong winter storm moved through the upper Midwest over the weekend, providing the Northland with its first widespread snowfall of the winter season. Heavy snow combined with strong winds to create blizzard conditions near Lake Superior, and near blizzard conditions elsewhere. The storm was preceded by mild weather, with temperatures in the 30s and 40s. A period of rain and freezing rain fell on Saturday evening (the 13th) across central and eastern Minnesota from Brainerd through Hinckley. ||Eight to 15 inches of snow fell across northeast Minnesota, with the highest amounts in the Iron Range, north to the Canadian border. Lesser amounts of 8 inches fell in the Brainerd Lakes region, towards Hinckley, and also in the tip of the Arrowhead. Some specific totals include 10 inches at Duluth and Remer, 12 inches at Chisholm, and 14 inches near Bigfork. ||Snowfall was heaviest across the region on Sunday afternoon (the 14th), accumulating 1 to 2 inches per hour. Winds gusting over 50 mph were common in Duluth, causing blizzard conditions with near zero visibility for several hours. The strong northeast winds caused large waves nearly 15 feet high at the Duluth ship entry. These waves damaged part of the Duluth Lakewalk. Bitterly cold wind chills in the 40s to near 50 below zero followed the storm.

A potent surface low pressure system moved out of Colorado late Saturday (13th) and tracked northeast to the Minneapolis area by noon on Sunday (14th). This created a strong temperature gradient across the northern plains, with Devils Lake (ND) at 15 below zero and the Minneapolis (MN) area around 30 above by noon Sunday. As the system intensified over eastern Minnesota, northwest winds began to gust to around 50 mph with wind chills colder than 40 below zero. Quite a bit of snow also accompanied the wind, which created whiteout conditions for an extended period of time. A blizzard this bad had not been seen since the winter of 1996/97, so the impact on the area was tremendous. Stores closed for portions of the weekend during the busy holiday shopping season. Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown (ND) to Alexandria (MN). U.S. Highway 10 was closed from Moorhead to Detroit Lakes and U.S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston. No travel was advised across the area. Church services, schools, and many other activities were cancelled or delayed. There were other minor power outages across the area as well.

An arctic cold front and a strong area of low pressure that developed across the northern Rockies and moved eastward across the upper Midwest and into the northern Great Lakes, brought heavy snow, very strong winds and blizzard conditions across a large portion of western and central Minnesota. A broad area of six inches (Locally (9+ inches occurred near the communities of Little Falls and Hewitt in central Minnesota) or more fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota on Sunday December 15th. The heaviest snowfall occurred Sunday morning where periods of very heavy snowfall fell across a large area of west central and central Minnesota. In addition to the snowfall, severe wind chills of 30 to 50 below zero occurred across the region as wind speeds increased and temperatures dropped well below zero. The hardest hit areas were across west central Minnesota where significant snowfall and wind gusts in excess of 40 mph produced blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the western part of Lac Qui Parle county between 9 and 10 pm Saturday (December 13th )evening as low pressure started to intensify across the northern Plains. Light snow continued through the night and became heavy between 7 am and 10 am on Sunday. In addition, northwest winds began to increase with sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts of 45 to 55 mph through Sunday evening. During the overnight hours of Sunday/Monday December 14/15, between midnight and 9 am, wind chill values averaged between 35 to 45 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved through the upper Midwest over the weekend, providing the Northland with its first widespread snowfall of the winter season. Heavy snow combined with strong winds to create blizzard conditions near Lake Superior, and near blizzard conditions elsewhere. The storm was preceded by mild weather, with temperatures in the 30s and 40s. A period of rain and freezing rain fell on Saturday evening (the 13th) across central and eastern Minnesota from Brainerd through Hinckley. ||Eight to 15 inches of snow fell across northeast Minnesota, with the highest amounts in the Iron Range, north to the Canadian border. Lesser amounts of 8 inches fell in the Brainerd Lakes region, towards Hinckley, and also in the tip of the Arrowhead. Some specific totals include 10 inches at Duluth and Remer, 12 inches at Chisholm, and 14 inches near Bigfork. ||Snowfall was heaviest across the region on Sunday afternoon (the 14th), accumulating 1 to 2 inches per hour. Winds gusting over 50 mph were common in Duluth, causing blizzard conditions with near zero visibility for several hours. The strong northeast winds caused large waves nearly 15 feet high at the Duluth ship entry. These waves damaged part of the Duluth Lakewalk. Bitterly cold wind chills in the 40s to near 50 below zero followed the storm.

Round two of two powerful winter storms plagued the upper Midwest in less than 48 hours as another fast moving system brought substantial snowfall, low temperatures, blizzard conditions and dangerously cold wind chills values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Waseca County after 5 am. After 11 am, snow became heavier, with periods of heavy snow in the late morning and early afternoon. The winter storm continued through the afternoon, but snowfall became lighter and surface visibilities improved.

This arctic front brought bitterly cold temperatures and dangerously cold winds chills of -40 to -50 degrees throughout northeast Minnesota. The lowest wind chill readings were recorded early Tuesday morning, and include -52 at Longville, -45 at Babbitt, -42 in Duluth and International Falls, and -41 at Brainerd and Grand Marais.

Strong winds caused blizzard conditions over southwest Minnesota, mainly away from the southern border of the state. The strong winds combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall. The new snowfall varied mostly from 2 to 4 inches. Zero visibilities and drifting snow made travel virtually impossible. Wind chill readings were driver to as low as 40 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds sustained around 30 mph with gusts over 40 mph combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of 2 inches or less to produce blowing snow, with brief visibilities around a quarter mile in places. Temperatures falling to near or a little below zero combined with the strong winds to produce wind chill readings of 20 to 35 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the central Plains toward the Ohio Valley and produced widespread snow across the Upper Midwest on January 21. In southeast Minnesota, highest snowfall totals reached around 8 inches. Specific reports from weather observers included 8.2 inches near La Crescent (Houston County) and 8.0 inches near Houston (Houston County).

No travel was advised across the area. Church services, schools, and many other activities were cancelled or delayed. There were other minor power outages across the area as well.

A potent surface low pressure system moved out of Colorado late Saturday (13th) and tracked northeast to the Minneapolis area by noon on Sunday (14th). This created a strong temperature gradient across the northern plains, with Devils Lake (ND) at 15 below zero and the Minneapolis (MN) area around 30 above by noon Sunday. As the system intensified over eastern Minnesota, northwest winds began to gust to around 50 mph with wind chills colder than 40 below zero. Quite a bit of snow also accompanied the wind, which created whiteout conditions for an extended period of time. No travel was advised across the area. Church services, schools, and many other activities were cancelled or delayed. There were other minor power outages across the area as well.

A potent winter weather system organized in the central plains Monday morning December 8th, and moved rapidly northeast across the Midwest by Tuesday morning December 9th. The combination of gulf moisture, increasing wind speeds and localized high snowfall rates, portions of south central Minnesota received between four and six inches of snowfall and sustained wind speeds of 20 to 30 mph during the height of the snowstorm late Monday night into Tuesday morning. Local observations near Blue Earth in Faribault County, Waseca in Waseca County, Albert Lea In Freeborn County and Owatonna in Steele County had sustained winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts over 30 mph at times during the early morning of Tuesday December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across Waseca County between 4 and 5 pm Monday afternoon, and became heavier during the evening and overnight period. Wind speeds increased significantly after 1 am with frequent gusts of 30 mph during the morning. Locally three to four inches fell around the county, but due to significant wind speeds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts over 35 mph, near blizzard conditions were felt along with considerable blowing and drifting of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Round two of two powerful winter storms plagued the upper Midwest in less than 48 hours as another fast moving system brought substantial snowfall, low temperatures, blizzard conditions and dangerously cold wind chills values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Renville County after midnight and quickly became heavy after 9 am. Wind speeds increased significantly during the late morning, with frequent gusts of 40 to 45 mph. The lowest surface visibilities and highest wind speeds occurred between 1030 am and 430 pm. The winter storm continued through the early evening, but snowfall became light and surface visibilities improved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved through the upper Midwest over the weekend, providing the Northland with its first widespread snowfall of the winter season. Heavy snow combined with strong winds to create blizzard conditions near Lake Superior, and near blizzard conditions elsewhere. The storm was preceded by mild weather, with temperatures in the 30s and 40s. A period of rain and freezing rain fell on Saturday evening (the 13th) across central and eastern Minnesota from Brainerd through Hinckley. ||Eight to 15 inches of snow fell across northeast Minnesota, with the highest amounts in the Iron Range, north to the Canadian border. Lesser amounts of 8 inches fell in the Brainerd Lakes region, towards Hinckley, and also in the tip of the Arrowhead. Some specific totals include 10 inches at Duluth and Remer, 12 inches at Chisholm, and 14 inches near Bigfork. ||Snowfall was heaviest across the region on Sunday afternoon (the 14th), accumulating 1 to 2 inches per hour. Winds gusting over 50 mph were common in Duluth, causing blizzard conditions with near zero visibility for several hours. The strong northeast winds caused large waves nearly 15 feet high at the Duluth ship entry. These waves damaged part of the Duluth Lakewalk. Bitterly cold wind chills in the 40s to near 50 below zero followed the storm.

An arctic cold front and a strong area of low pressure that developed across the northern Rockies and moved eastward across the upper Midwest and into the northern Great Lakes, brought heavy snow, very strong winds and blizzard conditions across a large portion of western and central Minnesota. A broad area of six inches (Locally (9+ inches occurred near the communities of Little Falls and Hewitt in central Minnesota) or more fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota on Sunday December 15th. The heaviest snowfall occurred Sunday morning where periods of very heavy snowfall fell across a large area of west central and central Minnesota. In addition to the snowfall, severe wind chills of 30 to 50 below zero occurred across the region as wind speeds increased and temperatures dropped well below zero. The hardest hit areas were across west central Minnesota where significant snowfall and wind gusts in excess of 40 mph produced blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds combined with temperatures in the teens below zero, caused wind chill values to fall below 30 below for several hours Sunday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved through the upper Midwest over the weekend, providing the Northland with its first widespread snowfall of the winter season. Heavy snow combined with strong winds to create blizzard conditions near Lake Superior, and near blizzard conditions elsewhere. The storm was preceded by mild weather, with temperatures in the 30s and 40s. A period of rain and freezing rain fell on Saturday evening (the 13th) across central and eastern Minnesota from Brainerd through Hinckley. ||Eight to 15 inches of snow fell across northeast Minnesota, with the highest amounts in the Iron Range, north to the Canadian border. Lesser amounts of 8 inches fell in the Brainerd Lakes region, towards Hinckley, and also in the tip of the Arrowhead. Some specific totals include 10 inches at Duluth and Remer, 12 inches at Chisholm, and 14 inches near Bigfork. ||Snowfall was heaviest across the region on Sunday afternoon (the 14th), accumulating 1 to 2 inches per hour. Winds gusting over 50 mph were common in Duluth, causing blizzard conditions with near zero visibility for several hours. The strong northeast winds caused large waves nearly 15 feet high at the Duluth ship entry. These waves damaged part of the Duluth Lakewalk. Bitterly cold wind chills in the 40s to near 50 below zero followed the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter weather system organized in the central plains Monday morning December 8th, and moved rapidly northeast across the Midwest by Tuesday morning December 9th. The combination of gulf moisture, increasing wind speeds and localized high snowfall rates, portions of south central Minnesota received between four and six inches of snowfall and sustained wind speeds of 20 to 30 mph during the height of the snowstorm late Monday night into Tuesday morning. Local observations near Blue Earth in Faribault County, Waseca in Waseca County, Albert Lea In Freeborn County and Owatonna in Steele County had sustained winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts over 30 mph at times during the early morning of Tuesday December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across Steele County between 4 and 5 pm Monday afternoon, and became heavier during the evening and overnight period. Wind speeds increased significantly after 2 am with frequent gusts of 30 to 35 mph during the morning. Locally four to six inches fell around the county, but due to significant wind speeds of 20 to 25 mph with gusts over 30 mph, near blizzard conditions were felt along with considerable blowing and drifting of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent surface low pressure system moved out of Colorado late Saturday (13th) and tracked northeast to the Minneapolis area by noon on Sunday (14th). This created a strong temperature gradient across the northern plains, with Devils Lake (ND) at 15 below zero and the Minneapolis (MN) area around 30 above by noon Sunday. As the system intensified over eastern Minnesota, northwest winds began to gust to around 50 mph with wind chills colder than 40 below zero. Quite a bit of snow also accompanied the wind, which created whiteout conditions for an extended period of time. A blizzard this bad had not been seen since the winter of 1996/97, so the impact on the area was tremendous. Stores closed for portions of the weekend during the busy holiday shopping season. Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown (ND) to Alexandria (MN). U.S. Highway 10 was closed from Moorhead to Detroit Lakes and U.S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston. No travel was advised across the area. Church services, schools, and many other activities were cancelled or delayed. There were other minor power outages across the area as well.

This is the time period where both surface visibilities were near one quarter of a mile and wind speeds were averaging around 30 mph with gusts of 40 to 45 mph. The winter storm continued through the afternoon, but snowfall became lighter and surface visibilities improved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure area moved out of the Rockies and across the Central Plains bringing widespread snow, blizzard conditions, and extreme winds chills to west central Minnesota. Snowfall amounts of 5 to 9 inches along with winds gusting over 50 mph caused widespread near zero visibilities and dangerous travel conditions. Wind chills fell into the 35 below to 45 below zero range. Most schools were closed on Monday due to the road conditions and extreme wind chills. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Artichoke Lake and Browns Valley; 7 inches at Ortonville; and 9 inches at Wheaton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front and a strong area of low pressure that developed across the northern Rockies and moved eastward across the upper Midwest and into the northern Great Lakes, brought heavy snow, very strong winds and blizzard conditions across a large portion of western and central Minnesota. A broad area of six inches (Locally (9+ inches occurred near the communities of Little Falls and Hewitt in central Minnesota) or more fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota on Sunday December 15th. The heaviest snowfall occurred Sunday morning where periods of very heavy snowfall fell across a large area of west central and central Minnesota. In addition to the snowfall, severe wind chills of 30 to 50 below zero occurred across the region as wind speeds increased and temperatures dropped well below zero. The hardest hit areas were across west central Minnesota where significant snowfall and wind gusts in excess of 40 mph produced blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds combined with temperatures in the single digits and teens below zero, caused wind chill values to fall below 30 below for several hours Sunday morning.

The hardest hit areas were across west central Minnesota where significant snowfall and wind gusts in excess of 40 mph produced blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the Sauk Center area between 6 and 8 pm Saturday (December 13th )evening as low pressure started to intensify across the northern Plains. Light snow continued through the night and became heavy between 5 am and 10 am on Sunday. Snowfall amounts ranged from two to four inches from Kimball to St. Cloud, to locally six inches in the western part of Stearns County near Sauk Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front and a strong area of low pressure that developed across the northern Rockies and moved eastward across the upper Midwest and into the northern Great Lakes, brought heavy snow, very strong winds and blizzard conditions across a large portion of western and central Minnesota. A broad area of six inches (Locally (9+ inches occurred near the communities of Little Falls and Hewitt in central Minnesota) or more fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota on Sunday December 15th. The heaviest snowfall occurred Sunday morning where periods of very heavy snowfall fell across a large area of west central and central Minnesota. In addition to the snowfall, severe wind chills of 30 to 50 below zero occurred across the region as wind speeds increased and temperatures dropped well below zero. The hardest hit areas were across west central Minnesota where significant snowfall and wind gusts in excess of 40 mph produced blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the St. Cloud area between 6 and 8 pm Saturday (December 13th )evening as low pressure started to intensify across the northern Plains. Light snow continued through the night and became heavy between 9 am and 11 am on Sunday. Snowfall amounts averaged between four and six inches but significant blowing and drifting of the snow, and dangerously cold wind chill values of 20 to 30 below zero continued throughout the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the Milaca area between 6 and 8 pm Saturday (December 13th )evening as low pressure started to intensify across the northern Plains. Light snow continued through the night and became heavy between 10 am and 12 pm on Sunday. Total snowfall amounts throughout the county averaged between five and nine inches, with the highest totals near Lake Mille Lacs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of the southern Plains on December 8, reaching the eastern Great Lakes by December 9. This system brought heavy snow to much of southeast Minnesota, with total accumulations of 6 to 9 inches common. Some of the highest totals included 9.0 inches at Rushford (Fillmore County), 8.5 inches at Dodge Center (Dodge County) and 8.0 inches near La Crescent (Houston County).

A surface low tracked across North Dakota and into north central Minnesota on Monday, January 28th. A strong surface pressure gradient set up over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota as the low pushed into southwest Ontario Monday night. Cold air rushed into the area by Tuesday morning, which dropped temperatures to five below to twenty below zero, with the coldest temperatures in the Devils Lake (ND) region. Temperatures remained steady or continued to fall during the day Tuesday, with wind chills ranging from 40 below to 50 below zero. Tuesday night lows fell to the 20s below zero, with the coldest lows again around the Devils Lake (ND) region. High pressure finally built into the area on Wednesday afternoon, which allowed wind speeds to relax. Some schools cancelled classes on Tuesday, while several other schools started 2 hours late. A woman was found dead from hypothermia five miles north of East Grand Forks after she had apparently walked away from her stranded vehicle early Wednesday morning.

Arctic air combined with strong northwest winds of 20 to 40 mph to bring extreme wind chills to west central Minnesota. The extreme wind chills began in the morning hours of January 29th and then improved  during the morning hours of January 30th. The extreme wind chills ranged from 35 to 45 degrees below zero across the area. The extreme cold caused school delays and activity cancellations along with much discomfort to people and livestock.||On Monday January 28th, the day before the extreme cold, a southerly flow brought very mild temperatures with highs in the lower 40s. When the Arctic front came through on January 28th, temperatures fell dramatically through the evening and early morning with below zero temperatures by Tuesday morning, January 29th. The temperature change from the 28th to the 29th across west central Minnesota was around 45 degrees.

Strong northwesterly winds, frequently gusting to 40-50 mph behind an arctic front, brought blizzard conditions to a small portion of southeast Minnesota. Some snow amounts of note include 2.50 inches in Wanamingo (Goodhue County), 2.0 inches five miles south of Geneva (Freeborn County) and also at Pine Island (Goodhue County). Low visibilities included: only one hundred feet outside of Owatonna, 20 feet or less at times in Geneva (according to Freeborn County Sheriff's Office), zero visibility reported just outside of Pine Island.  Southbound Interstate 35 was closed from Owatonna to Albert Lea.

A powerful winter storm moved from Missouri to lower Michigan on February 17. This system brought total snow accumulations of around 4 inches to a small part of southeast Minnesota near La Crosse, Wisconsin. Specific snowfall totals from volunteer weather observers included 3.8 inches near Reno. In addition to the snow, strong northwest winds gusting up to 30 to 35 mph caused hazardous traveling conditions.

A surface low tracked across North Dakota and into north central Minnesota on Monday, January 28th. A strong surface pressure gradient set up over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota as the low pushed into southwest Ontario Monday night. Cold air rushed into the area by Tuesday morning, which dropped temperatures to five below to twenty below zero, with the coldest temperatures in the Devils Lake (ND) region. Temperatures remained steady or continued to fall during the day Tuesday, with wind chills ranging from 40 below to 50 below zero. Strong winds and bitterly cold air combined to bring the lowest wind chill readings of the winter to northeastern Minnesota during the weekend of February 9-10. Wind chill values of -40 to -55 degrees Fahrenheit were common Saturday night and Sunday morning. Bitterly cold wind chills continued into Monday morning, the 11th, in the Arrowhead region. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were widespread across the Northland on the evening of the 9th. The lowest wind chill readings of the event occurred Sunday morning, the 10th, when Grand Marais dropped to -58, International Falls to -51, Hibbing and Grand Rapids to -49, and Duluth registered |-48.

Arctic air along with blustery northwest winds brought extreme wind chills during the evening and early morning hours to west central Minnesota. Wind chills ranged from 35 to 45 degrees below zero. The winds diminished in the early morning hours of the 20th allowing air temperatures to fall to record or near record lows across west central Minnesota. Record lows, ranging from 23 to 28 degrees below zero, were set for several locations on February 20th. There were many vehicles that did not start along with late school starts or closings.

Low pressure developed over western Nebraska and moved eastward into the Ohio Valley on March 21. Snow fell across much of southeast Minnesota, with heaviest accumulations between 6 and 9 inches near the Mississippi River. Volunteer weather observers reported 8.5 inches near Reads Landing (Wabasha County) and 6.0 inches near La Crescent (Houston County).

Wind chill values of -40 to -55 degrees Fahrenheit were common Saturday night and Sunday morning. Bitterly cold wind chills continued into Monday morning, the 11th, in the Arrowhead region. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were widespread across the Northland on the evening of the 9th. The lowest wind chill readings of the event occurred Sunday morning, the 10th, when Grand Marais dropped to -58, International Falls to -51, Hibbing and Grand Rapids to -49, and Duluth registered |-48.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across the Northern Plains brought snowfall, strong north winds, and bitter cold temperatures to west central Minnesota. Snowfall amounts of 2 to 8 inches occurred across the area. Northwest winds of 25 to 40 mph caused blizzard conditions and difficult, if not impossible, travel conditions. Some of the heavier snowfall amounts included: 5 inches at Wheaton and Ortonville; 6 inches at Artichoke Lake; 7 inches at Browns Valley and 8 inches at Tintah.

An arctic cold front swept across southeast Minnesota during the morning of January 29. This system was accompanied by snow, with highest accumulations of 2 to 5 inches mainly along and north of a line from Austin (Mower County) to Wabasha (Wabasha County). Strong northwest wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph caused considerable blowing snow. In fact, blizzard conditions were reported in some locations such as Dodge Center (Dodge County), Rochester (Olmsted County) and Austin (Mower County). Conditions became dangerous very quickly as the snow started and winds dramatically increased.  In Rochester, the temperature plummeted from 40F at midnight on the 29th to -12F shortly after 11 p.m. in the evening. This 52 degree drop tied a record for the sixth largest temperature change in a calendar day and was the largest temperature change in a calendar day since January 18, 1996.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to blizzard conditions, a multi-car pileup occurred around 12:30 p.m. on Interstate 90 between the Olmsted/Winona County line and Dover. Westbound lanes were closed to traffic at 1:35 p.m. and reopened at 4:15 p.m. There were no serious injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved from western Nebraska Thursday (20th) into southern Iowa Friday (21st) morning. The system developed bands of heavy snow up into southeastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota Thursday night into Friday. Six to seven inches of snow fell from Lisbon (ND) to Forman (ND) to Lidgerwood (ND) and into southern Wilkin County (MN) by the morning of Friday. Snow continued into Clay County (MN) to produce nearly 8 inches near the Fargo (ND)/Moorhead (MN) area by Friday evening.

Late in the evening of the 8th, a cold front was located along a line from the Turtle Mountains to just east of Bismarck (ND). By Saturday morning (9th), the front had moved into Minnesota, along a line from near Baudette to Detroit Lakes. Very little snow fell as the front moved through, as most locations reported an inch or less. However, north to northwest winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph behind the front, causing ground blizzard conditions in open country with wind chills from 25 below to 40 below zero. Snow plows were pulled in many areas, and some school events were cancelled. Interstate 94 was closed from Moorhead to Fergus Falls, U.S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston, and U.S. Highway 75 was closed in Polk County.

By the morning of the 10th, temperatures alone dropped to 20 below to 30 below zero. Surface high pressure moved over the area by noon, allowing wind speeds to fall below 10 mph.

A low pressure system moved from western Nebraska Thursday (20th) into southern Iowa Friday (21st) morning. The system developed bands of heavy snow up into southeastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota Thursday night into Friday. Six to seven inches of snow fell from Lisbon (ND) to Forman (ND) to Lidgerwood (ND) and into southern Wilkin County (MN) by the morning of Friday. Snow continued into Clay County (MN) to produce nearly 8 inches near the Fargo (ND)/Moorhead (MN) area by Friday evening.

A low pressure system pushed across the Central Plains Thursday and Friday (April 10th and 11th) and brought a variety of weather conditions to portions of east central and southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota. The event began with areas of light freezing rain on the 10th, then changed over to snow overnight. Snow accumulations were generally highest (6 to 13 inches) over areas that had also received heavy amounts of snow on April 6th. Strong winds also developed overnight from the 10th to the 11th, which caused significantly reduced visibilities in falling and blowing snow through the morning of the 11th. The snow and wind pushed east of the area by early afternoon.

An unusual late April winter storm hit the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest Friday (25th) and Saturday (26th). A low pressure system pushed northeast across Iowa Friday into Wisconsin Saturday. The storm blanketed much of far southeastern North Dakota and North and West Central Minnesota with more than 8 inches of snow. An area near Wahpeton, ND and Fergus Falls, MN got more than 12 inches. Hector International Airport broke its April 26th (Saturday) snowfall record, where 4.8 inches beat the old record of 3.5 inches set in 1937.

Late in the evening of the 8th, a cold front was located along a line from the Turtle Mountains to just east of Bismarck (ND). By Saturday morning (9th), the front had moved into Minnesota, along a line from near Baudette to Detroit Lakes. Very little snow fell as the front moved through, as most locations reported an inch or less. However, north to northwest winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph behind the front, causing ground blizzard conditions in open country with wind chills from 25 below to 40 below zero. Snow plows were pulled in many areas, and some school events were cancelled. Interstate 94 was closed from Moorhead to Fergus Falls, U.S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston, and U.S. Highway 75 was closed in Polk County.

Strong low level flow of 50 to 60 knots ahead of a passing low pressure system combined with light showers caused high wind gusts across eastern North Dakota and northwestern Minnesota the evening of Saturday March 29th. A sixty-five mph wind gust was reported that evening in Norman County where one-third of the siding was torn off the south side of a garage and the door was damaged. A fifty-nine mph wind gust was reported that evening on the Red Lake and Polk County lines which was measured by a MNDOT RWIS Mesonet site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This wind gust was reported by a MNDOT RWIS Mesonet on the Polk and Red Lake County lines.

An area of surface low pressure moved across South Dakota late on Saturday, April 5th, into Sunday, April 6th. A nearly stationary frontal boundary set up from this low northeast toward Saint Cloud, Minnesota. Precipitation across portions of southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota began as rain, then changed over to bands of snow. By Sunday morning, 8 to 12 inches of snow had occurred along a line from northern Richland County (ND) toward southern Hubbard County (MN). Snow continued through much of the day Sunday, so that by early evening, a corridor from Otter Tail County to Hubbard County picked up another 6 to 10 inches of snow. Some areas across southern Beltrami, southern Clearwater, and Hubbard counties received about 2 feet of snow.

A nearly stationary frontal boundary set up from this low northeast toward Saint Cloud, Minnesota. Precipitation across portions of southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota began as rain, then changed over to bands of snow. By Sunday morning, 8 to 12 inches of snow had occurred along a line from northern Richland County (ND) toward southern Hubbard County (MN). Snow continued through much of the day Sunday, so that by early evening, a corridor from Otter Tail County to Hubbard County picked up another 6 to 10 inches of snow. Some areas across southern Beltrami, southern Clearwater, and Hubbard counties received about 2 feet of snow.

Strong low level flow of 50 to 60 knots ahead of a passing low pressure system combined with light showers caused high wind gusts across eastern North Dakota and northwestern Minnesota the evening of Saturday March 29th. A sixty-five mph wind gust was reported that evening in Norman County where one-third of the siding was torn off the south side of a garage and the door was damaged. A fifty-nine mph wind gust was reported that evening on the Red Lake and Polk County lines which was measured by a MNDOT RWIS Mesonet site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One-third of the siding was torn off the south side of a garage and the door was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought periods of snow to much of east-central Minnesota. The highest amounts were in eastern Pine County where 6 to 7 inches of snow fell. The snow continued to fall into the morning of April 1.

Widepsread snow produced areas of heavy accumulations over southwest Minnesota during the predawn and daytime hours of March 31st. Accumulations varied from a little under 6 inches to a little over 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 5 inches. The snow and ice on roads contributed to a fatal vehicle accident on U.S. Highway 75 about 3 miles north of Luverne, where one person died and two others were injured in a head-on collision at about 10 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elderly woman was found dead lying outside her home at 12:11 pm. It is believed she fell, broke her hip and died of hypothermia. The low temperature in Brainerd that morning was -25F and the high temperature for the day was 14F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance coupled with an area of low pressure moving across the Central Plains brought widespread heavy snow during the nighttime and morning hours to west central Minnesota.  Heavy snow of 12 to 17 inches fell across this area resulting in school delays and cancellations along with treacherous travel conditions. Snowfall amounts included, 12 inches at Artichoke Lake, 13 inches at Browns Valley and Wheaton, and 17 inches at Ortonville.

A strong arctic cold front moved into central and southern Minnesota on January 1, dropping temperatures below zero.  A two year old boy in New Ulm wandered into the cold sometime Wednesday morning, January 2, and was found by his mother at 0745 CST, less than one block from home. He was pronounced dead at the New Ulm Medical Center.  The temperature at New Ulm airport dropped below zero around 0100 CST, and fell to eight below zero by 0700 CST.  The temperature warmed back above zero by 1000 CST.

Some storms became severe in the late afternoon and produced large hail. A waterspout touched down on Leech Lake and traveled approximately four miles in a northeasterly direction before dissipating.

A powerful late season winter storm hit northeastern Minnesota, producing heavy wet snow, with localized blizzard conditions along the North Shore and head of Lake Superior. Six to 14 inches of snow fell across a large part of the area, with 10 or more inches falling south of a line from Walker to Bigfork to Duluth. Isolated amounts of 14 inches fell across the Pillager and Brainerd areas. Much lighter amounts fell across the far north, including International Falls, which recorded nearly 2 inches of snow. Wind gusts over 40 mph were common across much of the area, with higher gusts near Lake Superior. Duluth International Airport recorded 62 mph, with 54 mph at Park Point in Duluth, and 51 mph at Two Harbors. Waves were 15 feet or more on Lake Superior. Power outages were widespread, with the majority of outages in the Duluth area. At one point, 11,000 Minnesota Power customers were without power. Many schools and businesses were closed on the 11th.

A powerful late season winter storm hit northeastern Minnesota, producing heavy wet snow, with localized blizzard conditions along the North Shore and head of Lake Superior. Six to 14 inches of snow fell across a large part of the area, with 10 or more inches falling south of a line from Walker to Bigfork to Duluth. Isolated amounts of 14 inches fell across the Pillager and Brainerd areas. Much lighter amounts fell across the far north, including International Falls, which recorded nearly 2 inches of snow. Wind gusts over 40 mph were common across much of the area, with higher gusts near Lake Superior. Duluth International Airport recorded 62 mph, with 54 mph at Park Point in Duluth, and 51 mph at Two Harbors. Waves were 15 feet or more on Lake Superior. Power outages were widespread, with the majority of outages in the Duluth area. At one point, 11,000 Minnesota Power customers were without power. Many schools and businesses were closed on the 11th.

An unusual late April winter storm hit the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest Friday (25th) and Saturday (26th). A low pressure system pushed northeast across Iowa Friday into Wisconsin Saturday. The storm blanketed much of far southeastern North Dakota and North and West Central Minnesota with more than 8 inches of snow. An area near Wahpeton, ND and Fergus Falls, MN got more than 12 inches. Gusty northerly winds produced blizzard and near blizzard conditions across West Central Minnesota, resulting in the closure of parts of Interstate 94 Saturday morning between Alexandria and Moorhead, MN. Travel was not advised in many other areas as well.

An area of low pressure moving across the Northern Plains spread heavy snow of 6 inches to west central Minnesota from the mid afternoon until the early morning hours. Strong winds gusting to over 40 mph caused some blowing and drifting snow. Some activities were cancelled and roads became treacherous. Also, some school openings were delayed on the 7th. Snowfall amounts included 6 inches at Browns Valley, Dumont, and Beardsley.

A strong low pressure area brought widespread heavy snow of 8 to 14 inches to west central Minnesota for much of the 25th and into the early morning hours of the 26th. The precipitation began as light freezing rain in the morning across parts of the area before changing to all snow by noon. As the low pressure area intensified, snowfall rates and the north winds also increased. The heavy snow combined with the strong winds created widespread visibility problems along with large snowdrifts. Snowfall amounts included, 8 inches at Artichoke Lake, 9 inches at Tintah, 10 inches at Wheaton, 11 inches at Ortonville, 12 inches at Browns Valley, and 14 inches at White Rock.   ||There were some automobiles that went into the ditch along with some other automobiles damaged in accidents. There were also stranded motorists. Travel was not advised across the entire area and many schools were closed early. Emergency shelters when opened to house stranded motorists.

All three were alert when rescue workers arrived. Two refused medical treatment and one person was taken to Hennepin County Medical Center with minor injuries. Information provided by Minneapolis Star Tribune newspaper.

Rain and freezing rain changed to snow in southwest Minnesota during the afternoon of April 10th. The snow became moderate to heavy at times and was accompanied by north winds averaging 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. This produced blizzard conditions from the Pipestone and Slayton areas north. The blizzard conditions continued into the early morning of April 11th. Traffic was brought to a standstill, and many schools and businesses were forced to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, beginning as rain and freezing rain, fell from just after noon on April 10th to the very early morning of April 11th. The snow accumulated 2 to 6 inches with the greatest accumulations over the northern part of the county. The snow was accompanied by north winds averaging around 30 mph and gusting to 45 mph. This produced blizzard conditions, with zero visibilities and drifting snow making travel impossible. Schools and numerous businesses were forced to close. A few power outages were reported as power lines fell from the weight of the wet snow and the strong winds.

Six to 14 inches of snow fell across a large part of the area, with 10 or more inches falling south of a line from Walker to Bigfork to Duluth. Isolated amounts of 14 inches fell across the Pillager and Brainerd areas. Much lighter amounts fell across the far north, including International Falls, which recorded nearly 2 inches of snow. Wind gusts over 40 mph were common across much of the area, with higher gusts near Lake Superior. Duluth International Airport recorded 62 mph, with 54 mph at Park Point in Duluth, and 51 mph at Two Harbors. Waves were 15 feet or more on Lake Superior. Power outages were widespread, with the majority of outages in the Duluth area. At one point, 11,000 Minnesota Power customers were without power. Many schools and businesses were closed on the 11th.

A strong low pressure area brought widespread heavy snow of 8 to 14 inches to west central Minnesota for much of the 25th and into the early morning hours of the 26th. The precipitation began as light freezing rain in the morning across parts of the area before changing to all snow by noon. As the low pressure area intensified, snowfall rates and the north winds also increased. The heavy snow combined with the strong winds created widespread visibility problems along with large snowdrifts. Snowfall amounts included, 8 inches at Artichoke Lake, 9 inches at Tintah, 10 inches at Wheaton, 11 inches at Ortonville, 12 inches at Browns Valley, and 14 inches at White Rock.   ||There were some automobiles that went into the ditch along with some other automobiles damaged in accidents. There were also stranded motorists. Travel was not advised across the entire area and many schools were closed early. Emergency shelters when opened to house stranded motorists.

Snow began on the evening of April 5 in much of north-central Minnesota and gradually spread eastward during the early morning hours. The snowfall briefly diminished in intensity during the midday hours of April 6, the became heavy later in the afternoon, before ending late the next morning. Snowfall amounts varied greatly, but generally 8 inches or more accumulated north of a line from Brainerd through Aitkin to Canyon to Grand Marais. A swath of 20 or more inches of snowfall was centered on a line from Cass Lake through the Iron Range to Crane Lake. The highest amounts included 25 inches at Cass Lake, 26 inches at Hibbing, and 32 inches just north of Virginia.

Rain and freezing rain changed to snow in southwest Minnesota during the afternoon of April 10th. The snow became moderate to heavy at times and was accompanied by north winds averaging 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. This produced blizzard conditions from the Pipestone and Slayton areas north. The blizzard conditions continued into the early morning of April 11th. Traffic was brought to a standstill, and many schools and businesses were forced to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, beginning as rain and freezing rain, fell from just after noon on April 10th to the very early morning of April 11th. The snow accumulated 2 to 7 inches with the greatest accumulations over the western part of the county. The snow was accompanied by north winds averaging around 30 mph and gusting to 45 mph. This produced blizzard conditions, with zero visibilities and drifting snow making travel impossible. Schools and numerous businesses were forced to close. Power outages were reported as power lines fell from the weight of the wet snow and the strong winds.

Snowfall amounts varied greatly, but generally 8 inches or more accumulated north of a line from Brainerd through Aitkin to Canyon to Grand Marais. A swath of 20 or more inches of snowfall was centered on a line from Cass Lake through the Iron Range to Crane Lake. The highest amounts included 25 inches at Cass Lake, 26 inches at Hibbing, and 32 inches just north of Virginia.

A surface low pressure system that moved from eastern Kansas on March 31st, to the northern peninsula of Michigan on the morning of April 1st, brought heavy snow to much of Central Minnesota. Some snowfall amounts of note include: 9.4 inches at New Hope; 8.3 inches in St Paul; 8.1 inches in Montgomery; 8.0 inches in New Brighton; 8.0 inches at West St. Paul; 7.5 inches in Glencoe; 7.5 inches at Rush City; 7.1 inches Maplewood; 6.0 inches at Cambridge; 6.0 inches in Milaca. Northwesterly winds in the 10 to 15 mph range also produced moderate blowing and drifting snow.

Rain and freezing rain changed to snow in southwest Minnesota during the afternoon of April 10th. The snow became moderate to heavy at times and was accompanied by north winds averaging 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. This produced blizzard conditions from the Pipestone and Slayton areas north. The blizzard conditions continued into the early morning of April 11th. Traffic was brought to a standstill, and many schools and businesses were forced to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, accompanied at the start by rain and freezing rain, fall from the afternoon of April 10th into the very early morning of April 11th. North winds increasing to 20 to 30 mph with gusts over 40 mph caused visibilities below a mile in the snow and blowing snow, temporarily making travel very difficult.

An intense area of low pressure moving northeast across the Central Plains brought widespread heavy snow and strong winds to west central Minnesota. Snowfall amounts from 3 to 14 inches combined with north winds of 25 to 45 mph brought widespread blowing snow with blizzard conditions and heavy drifting to the area. Some vehicles went into the ditch along with several accidents occurring. Schools were also delayed or closed. Snowfall amounts included, 3 inches at Tintah, 6 inches at Browns Valley, 8 inches at Artichoke Lake, 11 inches at Wheaton, and 14 inches at Ortonville.

A powerful late season winter storm hit northeastern Minnesota, producing heavy wet snow, with localized blizzard conditions along the North Shore and head of Lake Superior. Six to 14 inches of snow fell across a large part of the area, with 10 or more inches falling south of a line from Walker to Bigfork to Duluth. Isolated amounts of 14 inches fell across the Pillager and Brainerd areas. Much lighter amounts fell across the far north, including International Falls, which recorded nearly 2 inches of snow. Wind gusts over 40 mph were common across much of the area, with higher gusts near Lake Superior. Duluth International Airport recorded 62 mph, with 54 mph at Park Point in Duluth, and 51 mph at Two Harbors. Waves were 15 feet or more on Lake Superior. Power outages were widespread, with the majority of outages in the Duluth area. At one point, 11,000 Minnesota Power customers were without power. Many schools and businesses were closed on the 11th.
A warm frontal boundary moved from Central Iowa during the morning hours on the 1st, to far southern Minnesota by the evening hours. This boundary helped produce a few isolated severe thunderstorms.

Thunderstorms developed near a warm front situated along the Canadian border, while a cold front extended southward through central Minnesota. Some storms became severe in the late afternoon and produced large hail. A waterspout touched down on Leech Lake and traveled approximately four miles in a northeasterly direction before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout touched down on the south end of Leech Lake and travelled northeast for ten minutes before dissipating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on the eastern side of Lino Lakes, at the eastern shore of Peltier Lake. The tornado felled trees and lifted boat docks, then tracked east and crossed Interstate 35E. As this occurred, a few barns, grain bins, and silos were damaged. The tornado then intensified as it moved into northwestern Washington County and a subdivision (Creekview Preserve), about two miles north of downtown Hugo. It was at this point (around 16:01), that the twister caused EF-3 damage. The tornado dissipated in the eastern portion of Hugo, just north of Irish Avenue and 140th Street. Seven hundred and ninety four homes, including mobile homes, were affected by the storm in some way. A total of 91 homes sustained minor structural damage, numerous broken windows, and damage to small sections of roof. Twenty seven homes were destroyed, totally collapsed, or not economically feasible to repair. A two year old boy died after being blown out of the first floor of his home in Creekview Preserve. A 62 year old woman died an indirect death when she suffered a heart attack during cleanup in Hugo a few days later.  Some important information provided by the Pioneer Press, Star Tribune, and other local newspapers.

A vertically stacked cold-core low pressure system moved into the Red River Valley and brought ideal conditions for low topped mini supercells. A negatively tilted 500 mb trough, diffluent height field, and a strong upper level disturbance were all present. At the surface, there was even a warm front east of the low, toward Hubbard and Wadena Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado originated in Wadena County, 1 mile east of Menagha, at 914 am CDT. It continued north-northeast where it crossed Hinds Lakes and passed along the east edge of Park Rapids. It lifted about 3 miles northeast of Park Rapids by 934 am CDT. Maximum tornado width was about 450 yards with peak winds of 130 mph. The tornado destroyed one home and hundreds of acres of forest. Several homes and businesses from Hinds Lake to Park Rapids received damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported golf ball size hail in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vertically stacked cold-core low pressure system moved into the Red River Valley and brought ideal conditions for low topped mini supercells. A negatively tilted 500 mb trough, diffluent height field, and a strong upper level disturbance were all present. At the surface, there was even a warm front east of the low, toward Hubbard and Wadena Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down near the south end of Stocking Lake and tracked north-northeast for nearly 14 miles. It crossed into Hubbard County about 4 miles north-northeast of Menagha by 920 am CDT. It then tracked an additional 9 miles and lifted about 3 miles northeast of Park Rapids by 934 am CDT. The maximum tornado width was about 450 yards with peak winds of 130 mph. Eight large turkey barns were destroyed and several homes damaged along with hundreds of acres of forest. One man working near a turkey barn was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup for this event was much like the tornadic events which occurred on June 6th (2008), near Park Rapids, MN. A stacked low pressure system over eastern North Dakota produced a brief tornado touchdown in Norman County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down in the northeast corner of the intersection of Highway 10 and Main Street, and moved southeast. It lifted off in central Blaine, near the TPC Golf Club, just east of Highway 65 and north of 109th Avenue. Numerous trees were toppled or snapped, and some roof damage was also detected. One home was pushed off its foundation. A number of sheds and garages were destroyed. Two people in the parking lot at Kohl's in the Riverdale Village Shopping Center in Coon Rapids were picked up and blown over by the tornadic winds. In Blaine, one of the hardest hit areas was 118th Avenue, where extensive damage was incurred to several roofs, residential siding, and garages in the area. Several reports of damage were provided by the Coon Rapids Herald, the Blaine and Spring Lake Park Sun Focus, and other local newspapers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately three to five inches of heavy rain in three hours resulted in flash flooding. Water was reported over Highway 65 three miles southeast of Albert Lea, and over Interstate 90 five miles east and one mile north of Hayward.  Heavy rain washed out a section of County Road 34 between County Roads 19 and 21, or about three miles south of Oakland.  One man died when he drove into the washed out roadbed and the vehicle submerged. The first call to 911 came at 0005 CST June 12, alerting law enforcement that the road had washed out. Shortly thereafter, another call to 911 was placed when a vehicle with two occupants drove into the washed out roadbed.  The two occupants were rescued but informed their rescuers that they had landed on a vehicle, which turned out to be the vehicle with the deceased 52 year old male.  Two people died indirect deaths when, at night on June 29, at the same washed out location three miles south of Oakland, they either drove around a barricade or somebody had moved the barricade. The vehicle plunged into the cavernous hole. One woman died June 29 and a man died July 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rains fell in a short period of time across Cook and Lake Counties between about midnight and 6 am. The majority of the rain fell close to the Lake Superior shore centered near Grand Marais. A federal disaster declaration was declared for Cook County due to the  flooding. Thunderstorms tracking over the same areas for several hours led to the flash flooding, closing roads due to wash outs and debris on roadways, and leaving some homes and businesses flooded. Rainfall rates between one and two inches per hour led to the flash flooding, with rain totals between 4 and 6 inches common across southern Cook County and eastern Lake County by the time the rain ended in the morning. The bayside area of Grand Marais recorded 5.10 inches of rain, which is the second highest daily rain total, with records dating back to 1900.
Nickel size hail fell in west central Minnesota from an isolated thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across the northwestern quarter of Minnesota Thursday (12th) evening, ahead of a stacked low pressure system over northwest North Dakota. The best instability occurred to the east of the Red River Valley, along a surface trough. Cold temperatures aloft helped to contribute to the instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large four to six inch diameter branches were knocked down by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended east to west across the Upper Mississippi Valley on June 7, which provided the focus for thunderstorms producing excessive rainfall. In fact, these storms exhibited very high rainfall rates, which led to 1 to 2 inch rainfall amounts in an hour or so. As the storms congealed into a larger scale line of thunderstorms, they continued to move over the same areas, which led to significant flash flooding through the evening and overnight. ||On June 8, the warm front was lingering across the region, but a cold front was quickly tracking east out of the northern Plains. Showers and storms would redevelop during the day in the vicinity of the warm front, while a line of storms developed out ahead of the cold front, with these tracking across the region Sunday night. Again, copious amounts of moisture lead to periods of heavy rain. ||The heavy rainfall on June 8 enhanced the already dangerous flooding conditions across parts of southeast Minnesota. Many roads were already closed from the June 7 rains due to water over the roadways, mudslides, or partial washouts. Conditions only worsened, leading to more road closures, sandbagging, and some evacuations. ||While the rain was tapering off and moving east Sunday night, June 8, the rivers continued to rise, and some extremely quickly. Some rivers responded with a foot per hour rises. A top 5 record crest was set at the South Branch of the Root River at Preston.||Fillmore, Houston and Mower County were declared federal disaster areas. Damage to crops and infrastructure in these locations was preliminarily estimated at 25 to 35 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported over the southern half of the county. Roads that were closed due to high water included Highway 63 in Spring Valley and County Highway 15. A rainfall total of 7.25 inches was recorded for the weekend of June 7-8 in Harmony where over 100 basements flooded. There were nearly 300 homes impacted in the county along with contaminated or collapsed wells.  Other county roads closed due to high water included 17, 12, 30, and 8.  In Preston, 12 business were affected and the city waste water treatment plant was flooded.  Some evacuations were needed in Preston, along with Lanesboro.

A vertically stacked cold-core low pressure system moved into the Red River Valley and brought ideal conditions for low topped mini supercells. A negatively tilted 500 mb trough, diffluent height field, and a strong upper level disturbance were all present. At the surface, there was even a warm front east of the low, toward Hubbard and Wadena Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vertically stacked cold-core low pressure system moved into the Red River Valley and brought ideal conditions for low topped mini supercells. A negatively tilted 500 mb trough, diffluent height field, and a strong upper level disturbance were all present. At the surface, there was even a warm front east of the low, toward Hubbard and Wadena Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado tracked for about 7 miles from the southwest edge of Big Sand Lake, across Pickerel Lake, to 1 mile northwest of Emmaville by 947 am CDT. Maximum width was around 400 yards with peak winds to 160 mph. The tornado completely destroyed two homes and damaged several others on Pickerel Lake. It flattened dozens of acres of forest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado resulted from a storm split near Big Sand Lake and tracked to the northeast for nearly 5 miles. It had several brief touchdowns before it lifted about 5 miles east-southeast of Emmaville and east of Mantrap Lake by 951 am CDT. No significant damage was reported or detected by aerial survey, though some tree branch damage was noted. Estimated maximum width was 75 yards with peak winds estimated to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain with isolated thunder developed across southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota Monday (11th) morning ahead of an approaching cold front from the west. The rain continued into the afternoon and early evening, saturating the ground with two to three inches of rain. Another surge of precipitation developed Monday evening over eastern Richland County (ND) and central and northern Wilkin County (MN). This second surge of rain dumped an additional 3 to 6 inches of rain in a mere two to four hours. Flash flooding developed in the cities of Wahpeton (ND) and Breckenridge (MN), as well as several other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The city park was flooded and several streets were blocked off due to street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended east to west across the Upper Mississippi Valley on June 7, which provided the focus for thunderstorms producing excessive rainfall. In fact, these storms exhibited very high rainfall rates, which led to 1 to 2 inch rainfall amounts in an hour or so. As the storms congealed into a larger scale line of thunderstorms, they continued to move over the same areas, which led to significant flash flooding through the evening and overnight. ||On June 8, the warm front was lingering across the region, but a cold front was quickly tracking east out of the northern Plains. Showers and storms would redevelop during the day in the vicinity of the warm front, while a line of storms developed out ahead of the cold front, with these tracking across the region Sunday night. Again, copious amounts of moisture lead to periods of heavy rain. ||The heavy rainfall on June 8 enhanced the already dangerous flooding conditions across parts of southeast Minnesota. Many roads were already closed from the June 7 rains due to water over the roadways, mudslides, or partial washouts. Conditions only worsened, leading to more road closures, sandbagging, and some evacuations. ||While the rain was tapering off and moving east Sunday night, June 8, the rivers continued to rise, and some extremely quickly. Some rivers responded with a foot per hour rises. A top 5 record crest was set at the South Branch of the Root River at Preston.||Fillmore, Houston and Mower County were declared federal disaster areas. Damage to crops and infrastructure in these locations was preliminarily estimated at 25 to 35 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed due to high water, especially over the south part of the county near the Minnesota-Iowa state line. Creeks ran very high in Austin area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain with isolated thunder developed across southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota Monday (11th) morning ahead of an approaching cold front from the west. The rain continued into the afternoon and early evening, saturating the ground with two to three inches of rain. Another surge of precipitation developed Monday evening over eastern Richland County (ND) and central and northern Wilkin County (MN). This second surge of rain dumped an additional 3 to 6 inches of rain in a mere two to four hours. Flash flooding developed in the cities of Wahpeton (ND) and Breckenridge (MN), as well as several other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 75 was flooded at the railroad underpass near Kent.

A slow moving surface cold front moved from eastern North Dakota into western Minnesota early on the morning of the 2nd. Once again, a very moist airmass was in place ahead of the front, with dew points in the mid to upper 60s. The low level jet, moving straight north into the northern plains, focused precipitation along and behind the surface cold front. This resulted in flash flooding over portions of Richland (ND) and Wilkin (MN) counties.

Two minor injuries occurred at a turkey barn 2.5 miles South of Downtown Willmar.

A stationary front extended from central Minnesota into central Wisconsin on July 16. Thunderstorms developed and remained nearly stationary over extreme southeast Minnesota during the evening hours. This produced very heavy rainfall of up to four inches in La Crescent. Flooding and mudslides were reported in parts of Houston and Winona counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Consecutive thunderstorms hit extreme northeast parts of Houston County causing flash flooding and quick rises on Pine Creek.  Flash flooding was also reported in La Crescent.

A strong upper level low pressure system dived out of Canada and into the western Dakotas on Thursday (14th). Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern North Dakota and northwestern Minnesota Thursday afternoon and evening near an inverted trough, which extended north into the area from a surface low pressure system in Nebraska. The slow moving storms produced heavy rainfall amounts in some areas as well as some hail.

A strong upper level disturbance triggered strong and severe thunderstorms across much of northeast Minnesota during the morning and early afternoon hours.||By mid afternoon, very warm and moist air surged north ahead of low pressure over the Dakotas, while a cold front stretched across central Minnesota. This combination of meteorological conditions set the stage for additional severe thunderstorm development through the evening.||Storms developed in a broken line in western Minnesota by mid afternoon and propagated eastward through the late afternoon into the Brainerd Lakes region. By this time, a nearly solid line of thunderstorms extended from central Minnesota northward almost to the Canadian border. Many reports of wind damage and hail were noted with these storms as they moved east.||One particular area of wind damage was concentrated in northern Aitkin County, north of McGregor. Tree damage was widespread along a 6-mile path, from about 3 miles east of Palisade, to the south end of Big Sandy Lake. Results of a NWS storm survey assessment suggested that straight line and downburst winds were the primary cause. The majority of trees were blown down in one direction or fanned out in a divergent pattern. Many of the trees downed were over one foot in diameter, and some as large as two feet in diameter.||Thousands of trees in a 420 acre area of St. Croix State Park were mangled or uprooted. This area will be logged to mitigate wildfires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance triggered strong and severe thunderstorms across much of northeast Minnesota during the morning and early afternoon hours.||By mid afternoon, very warm and moist air surged north ahead of low pressure over the Dakotas, while a cold front stretched across central Minnesota. This area will be logged to mitigate wildfires.

A front remained nearly stationary from southwest Wisconsin into western Minnesota on July 10th. An upper level disturbance tracked along the front, triggering a line of severe thunderstorms over southeast Minnesota. Hail up to 2 inches in diameter and downed trees were reported by storm spotters and law enforcement officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported in addition to a measured wind gust of 53 miles per hour at the Emergency Operations Center.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown and blocked parts of Highway 30.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado tracked to the north-northeast for about 16 miles and lifted about 3 miles south-southwest of Lengby, or near Sugar Bowl Lake. Numerous trees and power poles were snapped along the path. Farm sheds were blown down and hay wagons flipped. The damage path was around 250 yards wide where it crossed county road 122 about 2 miles east of Beaulieu. Peak winds were estimated at 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm frontal boundry which moved across Southern and Central Minnesota was the focus for scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms across the region on this date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One hundred thousand square foot building under contruction destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced numerous large hail reports, as well as some flash flooding and damaging winds, in southwest Minnesota during the early afternoon of June 5th.

Rainfall amounts of 6 to 8 inches that occurred during the weekend of June 7-8 caused flooding June 12-13 along area creeks and rivers around Austin. In fact, top 5 record crests were set at Dobbins and Turtle Creek, as well as the Cedar River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River in Austin reached a crest of 22.4 feet at the waste water treatment plant and 21.4 feet 3 miles south of Austin. Both of these crests occurred on June 12 and were the third highest on record. Flood stage at the waste water treatment plant is 15.0 feet and 11.0 feet 3 miles south of Austin. In addition, Turtle and Dobbins Creek near Austin crested at 13.74 feet and 18.95 feet respectively on June 12, which were both the second highest crests on record. All-time record crests were 14.77 feet at Turtle Creek, 25.0 feet at the waste water treatment plant and 23.26 feet 3 miles south of Austin, all of which occurred on September 16, 2004. Dobbins Creek all-time record crest was 19.18 feet on July 10, 2000.||Sections of Interstate 90 were closed from flood waters along the Cedar River.
